text: The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum and places ports using the XI robot. The omentum is flipped above the transverse colon to reflect it, exposing the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum and places robotic ports in a typical configuration using the XI robot. The omentum is flipped above the transverse colon, reflecting the transverse mesocolon to open up the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to take down adhesions laparoscopically, addressing the hindrance caused by the adhesions in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses robotic instrumentation to take down adhesions, detaching the omental attachment. The tip-up grasper in arm number four is then used to unfold the retroperitoneum.

The surgeon uses robotic instrumentation, specifically the tip-up grasper, to detach the omental attachment and unfold the retroperitoneum. The grasper then pulls the transverse colon towards the patient's left upper quadrant.

The surgeon uses the tip-up fenestrated bipolar grasper to grasp the mesocolon of the transverse colon, pulling it towards the patient's left upper quadrant to unfold and open the retroperitoneal space.

The surgeon uses the third arm, a tip-up fenestrated grasper, to grasp the mesocolon of the transverse colon and pull it towards the left upper quadrant, effectively unfolding the retroperitoneum. The second portion of the duodenum is visible, along with the ilicoid pedicle, which is held up by the cyst towards the patient's right lower quadrant, stretching the ilicoid pedicle.

The surgeon identifies the second portion of the duodenum and the ilicoid pedicle, using a cyst to hold the ilicoid pedicle towards the patient's right lower quadrant. The video highlights anatomical landmarks, including the duodenum and the ilicoid pedicle, with a visible reflection of light in a small peritoneal sulcus.

The surgeon identifies anatomical landmarks, including the duodenum and the ilicoid pedicle, highlighting a reflective sulcus in the peritoneum. This sulcus marks the starting point for the medial to lateral dissection, where the incision is made.

The surgeon begins the medial to lateral dissection by making a clean peritoneal incision using cautery in the cut setting, minimizing bleeding. The blunt dissection in the retroperitoneal plane reveals the duodenum, which becomes visible within one to two centimeters on the medial portion of the dissection. The surgeon then encircles and divides the ovococcal pedicle.

The surgeon begins the medial to lateral dissection using cautery on the cut setting to minimize bleeding, creating a clean peritoneal incision. The retroperitoneal plane is bluntly dissected, revealing the duodenum within one to two centimeters on the medial portion of the dissection. The duodenum becomes visible on the patient's left side, and the surgeon encircles and divides the ovococcal pedicle.
The surgeon encircles the iliacolic pedicle, creating a window above it. Using a vessel sealer, the surgeon seals and divides the vessel while ensuring the duodenum remains positioned below.

The surgeon encircles the iliacolic pedicle, creating a window above it. Using a vessel sealer, the surgeon seals the vessel multiple times before dividing it, ensuring the duodenum remains undisturbed below.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to seal the iliacolic pedicle four times before cutting it. After division, the iliacolic pedicle is grasped and lifted anteriorly towards the abdominal wall.

The surgeon lifts the divided iliacolic pedicle anteriorly towards the abdominal wall, revealing the plane beneath. The pedicle is grasped and elevated, demonstrating the intended surgical plane, although the surgeon notes being slightly deep in the dissection.

The surgeon lifts the tissue plane anteriorly, revealing the dissection path towards the patient's right lower quadrant. The cecum is carefully peeled off the retroperitoneum, followed by the ascending colon, as the dissection progresses towards the liver. The goal is to create a defect in the hepatococcal ligament, allowing visualization of the gallbladder and liver medially, indicating completion of the medial dissection.

The surgeon peels the cecum off the retroperitoneum in the patient's right lower quadrant, then turns northward to peel the ascending colon off, progressing towards the liver. The dissection aims to create a defect in the hepatococcal ligament, revealing the gallbladder and liver medially, indicating completion of the medial dissection.

The surgeon uses the robotic XI system to adjust the camera angle for better visualization, lifting the hepatic flexure with the left hand while pushing down with the right hand to create a defect in the hepatic colic ligament. The lateral edge of the liver and the abdominal wall become visible as the dissection progresses retroperitoneally.

The surgeon uses the third arm of the robotic system for retraction while lifting the hepatic flexure with the left hand and pushing down with the right hand to create a defect in the hepatic colic ligament. The camera angle is adjusted from thirty degrees down to thirty degrees up to improve the view of the abdominal wall.

The surgeon visualizes the lateral edge of the liver and continues distal dissection by pushing down with the right hand. The third robotic arm lifts the transverse colon and omentum, revealing adhesions that are divided. The assistant pulls the hepatic flexure downward, exposing the defect created in the retroperitoneal plane.

The surgeon begins the subtotal colectomy by pushing down on the right side, progressing distally towards the patient's left. The middle colic artery is divided, and the dissection continues distally to reach the splenic flexure and enter the lesser sac, following a medial collateral approach.

The surgeon shifts focus to the lateral dissection, starting from the hepatic flexure down to the cecum. The third robotic arm lifts the omentum off the transverse colon, revealing unusual adhesions that are divided. The assistant pulls the hepatic flexure downward, exposing the defect created in the retroperitoneal plane.
The surgeon attempts to identify the large defect created during the retroperitoneal dissection, focusing on the anterior aspect to determine safe areas for further dissection.

The surgeon identifies a defect created in the retroperitoneal dissection, using a vessel sealer to safely take tissue to the left of the defect. The hook cautery is then used to dissect medially along the white line of Toldt, unfolding the right colon and mobilizing it down to the level of the cecum.

The surgeon inserts a vessel sealer into the identified defect, ensuring the area to the left is safe for dissection. The surgeon uses the vessel sealer to seal the tissue, followed by hook cautery to dissect medial to the white line of Toldt, unfolding and mobilizing the right colon down to the cecum.

The surgeon continues the mobilization of the right colon, focusing on the final attachments of the terminal ileum and cecum to the right lower quadrant. The appendix becomes visible as the dissection progresses towards the pelvis.

The surgeon addresses the final attachments of the terminal ileum and cecum in the right lower quadrant, with the appendix visible in the pelvic region. The third robotic arm, off-camera, provides counter-traction by pulling the hepatic flexure towards the patient's left upper quadrant, while the left hand, equipped with a bipolar instrument, actively retracts tissue.

The surgeon's third arm, off-camera, pulls the hepatic flexure towards the patient's left upper quadrant, providing counter-traction and macro-retraction. The left hand uses a bipolar instrument for dynamic retraction. The final terminal ileal attachments are visible, and the medial colonic dissection is completed, with the iliac vessels taken and the duodenum freed, finalizing the mobilization of the right colon.

The surgeon completes the medial dissection, freeing the duodenum and mobilizing the right colon. The terminal ileum and transverse colon are prepared for division. The colon is rotated laterally, and the divided ileocolic pedicle is lifted towards the patient's left shoulder using the third robotic arm.

The surgeon rotates the colon laterally to prepare for the division of the terminal ileum and transverse colon. The divided ileococcal pedicle is manipulated using the third arm, pulling it towards the patient's left shoulder, forming a visible V shape within the mesentery of the terminal ileum. The camera is briefly removed for cleaning before being reinserted to continue the procedure.

The surgeon uses the third arm to pull the mesentery of the terminal ileum towards the left shoulder, creating a visible V shape. After cleaning the camera lens, the vessel sealer is used to divide the terminal ileal mesentery with two burns, aiming towards the fold of Treves. The assistant then grabs and tents up the fold of Treves.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the terminal ileal mesentery, performing two burns aimed towards the fold of Treves. The assistant grabs the fold of Treves and tents it up, aligning the mesentery for precise division.

The surgeon aligns the mesentery of the terminal ileum, adjusting the angle and position to accommodate the specific needs of a Crohn's patient. The assist pulls the bowel up, allowing for precise division of the small bowel. The surgeon continues to divide the mesentery, progressively skeletonizing it down to the mesenteric border of the terminal ileum.

The surgeon adjusts the camera angle and directs the assistant to pull the bowel upwards, aligning it for optimal division. The small bowel is progressively divided until it is skeletonized down to the mesenteric border of the terminal ileum. A stapler is then brought in to complete the division.

The surgeon introduces a blue stapler to divide the colon, targeting the area just distal to the hepatic flexure in the patient's right upper quadrant. This division is performed to remove the right colon due to a cecal cancer, without extending to a right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon uses a blue stapler to divide the colon just distal to the hepatic flexure, targeting the area affected by cecal cancer. The procedure focuses on removing the right colon without extending to a right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon uses the third arm to grasp and elevate the transverse colon towards the patient's mid-epigastrum. The duodenum is visible and is pushed down to facilitate access. The mesentery of the transverse colon is divided using a vessel sealer.

The surgeon uses the third arm to grasp and elevate the transverse colon towards the patient's mid-epigastrum. The duodenum is visible and pushed down. The surgeon then divides the mesentery of the transverse colon using a vessel sealer, navigating through the dense mesentery and omentum.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the mesentery, navigating through the dense omentum and mesentery tissue. The instrument appears large due to magnification, but the surgeon takes liberal bites to progress through the transverse mesocolon.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to hold up the transverse colon at the point of division. The stapler is then brought in and positioned to include the tinea libra within its crotch. Firefly imaging is used to assess perfusion, revealing a discrepancy of about a centimeter from the intended stapling site.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to hold the transverse colon at the point of division. The stapler is brought in and aligned with the tinea libra within the crotch of the stapler. Firefly imaging is used to assess perfusion, revealing the stapler's position off by about a centimeter. The stapler is adjusted slightly to the patient's left for optimal placement before firing.

The surgeon opens and advances the stapler slightly to the patient's left, making a distal division on the colon. After completing the division, the specimen is parked above the right level of the liver in preparation for the anastomosis.
After completing the division, the surgeon positions the resected specimen above the right level of the liver, preparing the surgical field for the anastomosis.
